note: Session 2 taste checkpoint: the daily-rhythm cards (each card, then its left/right outcomes as separate swipes), week names, week-turn recap copy, tentpole trims, and the Connection rename lines. «guillemets» mark runtime tokens.

items.context: Morning · the kitchen · day four · daily beat (Arrival)
items.prompt: {partner} slides an iced coffee across the counter before you’ve said a word. “Oat, no sugar. I listen.” Four days in, and somebody has learned your order.
items.right: “Careful. I could get used to this”

items.text:
BAD: You’re so busy playing it cool the coffee sweats a full ring into the counter. “It’s fine,” says {partner}, in the voice of someone filing that away.

GOOD: You say nothing and drink it slowly, and {partner} pretends not to watch you drink it. The villa’s quietest transaction, and its realest one so far.

INCREDIBLE: Next morning there are two glasses out before either of you is awake enough to claim credit. A routine. The nation clocks it before you do.

items.context: li_iced_coffee → ““Careful. I could get used to this””
items.text:
BAD: “Then get used to it,” {partner} says, too fast, and the kitchen goes quiet enough to hear the ice. You both laugh one beat late. The beat airs.

GOOD: “Don’t threaten me with a good time,” says {partner}, and the whole counter suddenly needs wiping. Flirting before nine. Strong scenes.

INCREDIBLE: The back-and-forth runs four rounds and ends with {partner} bowing out holding the blender for protection. The clip does numbers before lunch.

items.context: Six a.m. · the roof terrace · nobody else up · daily beat (Arrival)
items.prompt: You’re awake first, which never happens. On the terrace, {mate} is already sitting with two teas, one of them yours. “Couldn’t sleep either. Don’t tell anyone I do sincerity.”

items.text:
BAD: You’re mid-sentence about something true when the garden mics crackle on. “Save it,” {mate} says gently. You bank it instead. Even the quiet here has a schedule.

GOOD: Twenty minutes, no cameras worth performing for, one conversation you’d have had in the real world. Your head files a complaint, then quietly drops it.

INCREDIBLE: “You’re alright, you know,” {mate} says at the exact moment the sun clears the wall, and the show can’t use any of it because nothing happened. Perfect.

BAD: Your six a.m. read of the villa is forensic, ruthless — and delivered directly over a live mic pack. “Morning!” says a producer’s voice from nowhere.

GOOD: You map every couple in the villa over one tea. {mate} adds footnotes. By the time the others wake up, you two are the only people here with a plan.

INCREDIBLE: “Watch the new boy at breakfast,” {mate} says. You watch. It’s exactly as predicted, twice. The two of you clink mugs like analysts at full time.

items.context: Two p.m. · the lawn · the wobbly lounger · daily beat (Arrival)
items.prompt: There is one wobbly sun lounger, and a rota for avoiding it that nobody wrote down but everybody knows. {rival} has just moved your towel onto it. “Oh — was that yours?”
items.right: Move the towel back. Hold eye contact

items.text:
BAD: Your bit about the lounger’s tragic backstory runs long. The lawn drifts off to the pool mid-punchline, which is somehow worse than booing.

GOOD: You give the lounger a eulogy. “It wobbles because it has seen things.” The lawn cackles; {rival} has to laugh along at their own move. Point taken, no blood.

INCREDIBLE: By dinner the wobbly lounger has a name, a backstory, and a small cult. {rival}’s towel move is now the setup to YOUR running joke. Daylight robbery.

items.context: li_lounger_row → “Move the towel back. Hold eye contact”
items.text:
BAD: The towel goes back. So does theirs. Back. Theirs. Back. Fourteen seconds of silent laundry chess, and the villa watches all of it through sunglasses.

GOOD: “All yours,” you say, placing their towel on the wobbler with hospital corners. {rival} sits on it out of pure stubbornness. It wobbles. Justice, at 2 p.m.

INCREDIBLE: You don’t say a word. You just straighten your towel and lie back, and the lawn quietly reclassifies you as someone not to try that on. {rival} recalculates.

items.context: Evening · the dressing room · exhibit A · daily beat (Arrival)
items.prompt: It got cold on the terrace last night and now you own {partner}’s hoodie, in the sense that it is on your body and you have no plans. “Looks better on you,” they said. A trap, or a fact. Or both.

items.text:
BAD: You return it so promptly it reads as a verdict. “Right,” says {partner}, taking the world’s most politely rejected hoodie. You meant it nicely. It didn’t land nicely.

GOOD: “You washed it?” {partner} says, holding it like evidence of something. “Nobody washes it.” You have accidentally set a precedent and a standard.

INCREDIBLE: It comes back washed with a note in the pocket — one line, private, not for the cameras. {partner} reads it twice and doesn’t tell anyone. The villa hates a secret it can’t see.

BAD: You wear it to breakfast the same morning {rival} decides breakfast needs commentary. “Cute. Whose is it again?” The table already knows. That’s the point of the question.

GOOD: You say nothing about it, which says everything about it. {partner} clocks it across the table and misses their mouth with the spoon. Breakfast television.

INCREDIBLE: The hoodie becomes a headline. The nation screenshots the sleeve. Somewhere a merch intern opens a spreadsheet. {partner} has never looked so pleased to be robbed.

items.context: Four a.m. · the kitchen · one lamp on · daily beat (The Turn)
items.prompt: You come down for water and {partner} is already there, sitting on the counter in the dark. “Couldn’t switch it off,” they say, tapping their temple. “Don’t make it a thing.”

items.text:
BAD: You open with “talk to me” and {partner} closes like a shop. “I said don’t make it a thing.” You made it a thing. The fridge hums judgment until you both give up.

GOOD: You don’t ask. You just get up on the counter next to them and wait. Third minute in, they start talking. Turns out the counter was the therapy all along.

INCREDIBLE: At some point they stop mid-sentence and go, “why is this easy with you?” — and that, at 4 a.m., unmiked and unplanned, is the whole show. Nobody else will ever see it.

BAD: The smoke alarm has opinions about your toastie. Lights on, villa up, {mate} downstairs in a towel demanding names. The moment, whatever it was, is now a fire drill.

GOOD: You make two, cut them wrong on purpose, and argue about the cutting instead of the thing. By the last bite {partner}’s shoulders have come down an inch. Cheese, doing therapy’s job.

INCREDIBLE: “You’re the only person here who’d feed me instead of interviewing me,” {partner} says, mouth full. The bar is on the floor and you have still, somehow, won the night.

items.context: After the ceremony · the firepit · everyone performing fine-ness · daily beat (The Turn)
items.prompt: The fire pit is doing its post-ceremony theatre — everyone laughing one notch too loud. Across the flames, {mate} catches your eye and mouths, “you good?” The first person all night to ask.

items.text:
BAD: You get one honest sentence out before the group descends mid-word, and you convert the feeling into a joke on the fly. {mate} clocks the swerve. “Later,” they mouth. Later never quite comes.

GOOD: “Honestly? No.” Two minutes by the fire, no performance, one person listening. It fixes nothing and helps enormously — the villa’s only genuine currency exchange.

INCREDIBLE: {mate} hears you out, nods once, and starts running interference — steering the loud ones away all night without being asked. Somewhere in here you acquired actual backup.

items.context: li_you_good → ““All good.” Keep the mask on”
items.text:
BAD: “All good,” you say, to the one person in the villa who can tell it isn’t. {mate} lets it go, visibly. The mask holds. The head, underneath it, does not.

GOOD: You keep it together and keep the group’s energy up, and the edit reads you as unbothered — which, at this stage of the season, is a currency of its own.

INCREDIBLE: Your fine-ness is so convincing that two separate Islanders come to YOU for reassurance. You dispense wisdom you do not currently possess. The camera loves a rock.

items.context: Mid-morning · the washing line · pegs and intelligence · daily beat (The Turn)
items.prompt: You’re pegging out swimwear with {mate} when two voices drift over the hedge, discussing a couple that is not theirs. Yours, possibly. The pegging slows to surveillance speed.

items.text:
BAD: You lean so far into the hedge for the ending that you wear it. Two heads pop round the other side. “Alright?” Nothing to do now but compliment their laundry.

GOOD: You hear the whole thing, file it, and peg on like the hedge said nothing. {mate} raises an eyebrow; you raise one back. A full conversation, zero words, one asset.

INCREDIBLE: Not only do you keep it — three days later it pays out, when someone tries a story on you that you already know ends differently. Your face doesn’t move. Theirs does.

BAD: Your retelling grows a detail in transit, the detail grows legs, and by dinner the story has your name on it as the source. The hedge, notably, stays anonymous.

GOOD: You deliver the hedge report to the daybeds with full dramatic staging. The villa redistributes its attention accordingly. You are, briefly, the six o’clock news.

INCREDIBLE: Your performance of “the hedge, verbatim” — two voices, one peg as a prop — becomes the villa’s clip of the day. Journalism is dead; this is better.

items.context: Dinner · the big table · callback incoming · daily beat (The Turn)
items.prompt: Somebody at dinner says the word “allegedly” and {partner} catches your eye across the table, already fighting a grin — it’s the thing, from the thing, that only you two were there for.

items.text:
BAD: You both lose it simultaneously into your drinks, and the table demands the joke. It doesn’t survive translation. “You had to be there” lands like a court summons.

GOOD: One look, one bitten lip each, nothing said. The table moves on. Under it, {partner}’s foot finds yours — the joke continuing by other means.

INCREDIBLE: You don’t even smile. Neither do they. The restraint is so total it becomes its own inside joke, layered on the first. You are now two jokes deep in a private language.

BAD: You tell the story. {partner} smiles with the front of their face. Some things are load-bearing precisely because they’re private, and you can hear this one crack.

GOOD: You tell it well, {partner} jumps in for the ending, and the double act is so smooth the table starts a rumour you rehearsed it. A couple with BITS. Dangerous.

INCREDIBLE: The story kills, the callback kills harder, and “allegedly” enters the villa dictionary with you two credited as authors. The nation adopts it within the hour.

items.context: Dawn · the roof terrace · the last quiet one · daily beat (Final Week)
items.prompt: “Get up,” {partner} whispers, at an hour that is technically a crime. “It’s the last one where nobody knows anything yet.” The terrace, two blankets, a sky going from grey to gold over a villa that ends this week.

items.text:
BAD: You try to hold the silence and your brain holds a recoupling instead. {partner} watches the sunrise; you watch the maths. They notice. Of course they notice.

GOOD: Neither of you says a word for twenty minutes, and it’s the best conversation you’ve had all week. Your head goes quiet on its own — no Beach Hut required.

INCREDIBLE: Somewhere mid-sunrise their hand finds yours without either of you deciding it. Whatever the envelope says on Friday, this bit already happened. The nation never gets it.

BAD: You open the out-there conversation at dawn with no exits. Trains, distances, whose city. By full daylight you’ve invented four problems you didn’t have at sunrise.

GOOD: “I’m an hour on the train,” {partner} says, like they’ve checked. They’ve checked. You talk about the real world like it’s a place you’re actually going. Together, apparently.

INCREDIBLE: They’ve already thought about the dog. Not a hypothetical dog — a breed, a name, a custody schedule for weekends. You laugh, then you don’t, because you’d also thought about the dog.

items.context: Final Week · the dressing room · suitcases out · daily beat (Final Week)
items.prompt: The suitcases are out on the beds, which makes it real in a way no ceremony has. {mate} holds up a single orphaned flip-flop. “Whose IS this? It’s been here since week one. It has seniority.”

items.text:
BAD: Folding gets philosophical, philosophical gets teary, and you lose an hour to “remember week one” while your case sits empty. The flip-flop watches, unclaimed.

GOOD: You fold, {mate} narrates, and the villa’s whole season gets sorted into keep, bin, and “burn before customs.” It’s an ending, done the manageable size.

INCREDIBLE: At the bottom of your case: the note, the bracelet from the challenge, a Mini Milk wrapper you kept on purpose. {mate} looks over. “Don’t,” you say. You both do.

BAD: The farewell runway peaks early when you go over on a heel that isn’t yours. The fall airs in slow motion. Twice. The physio has opinions about Final Week.

GOOD: Everyone models the season’s worst purchases. {mate} commentates in a towel turban. It’s the hardest the villa has laughed since the wobbly lounger, and it needed it.

INCREDIBLE: The finale look is the orphaned flip-flop, worn as a hat. The villa gives it a standing ovation and a name. Production quietly adds it to the tour of the villa.

WEEK 1 · Arrival
WEEK 2 · The Graft
WEEK 3 · Casa Amor
WEEK 4 · Movie Night
WEEK 5 · The Recoupling
WEEK 6 · Final Week

(Each week is named for the tentpole it builds to — the anticipation is on the scoreboard.)
PREVIOUSLY, IN THE VILLA · WEEK «n»

items.context: Week 1 → 2 recap · story, when a bombshell landed
items.text: And last night the doors opened: «Zara» unpacked a suitcase and a plan.
Week two was the graft: iced coffees, terrace debriefs, and a challenge with a scoreboard nobody asked for. At the Crossroads you said the quiet part into a microphone.
Week two: the couples stopped auditioning and started existing. Production made a note to fix that.
The grafting week is done — favours banked, orders memorised, a challenge survived on camera.
Movie Night came for everyone’s receipts and left yours, remarkably, alone.
Somewhere in the wreckage you acquired a ride-or-die — two spoons, one tub, terms binding.

items.context: Week 4 → 5 recap · story, the rival moved
items.text: «Chloe» stopped watching and made their move. You noticed. So did the nation.
The recoupling came and went, and you’re still holding a drink on the lawn. Which brings us here: the last week there is.

items.context: Week 5 → 6 recap · story, a body was taken
items.text: The firepit took a body. It nearly took yours. Which brings us here: the last week there is.
⚠️ The graft gets graded: a challenge at the end of the week, played for laughs and scored for keeps. And after it, the Crossroads — you declare why you’re really here, out loud, on the record.
⚠️ Casa Amor. At the end of this week the villa splits, strangers unpack, and loyalty gets a camera pointed at it. Whatever happens over there comes home on a postcard.
⚠️ Movie Night. The footage exists, and this week production presses play — footage outranks feelings, every time. New allies help. So does a clean reel, if yours is one.

items.context: Week 5 recap · THIS WEEK (you hold the pen / exposed)
items.text:
⚠️ This week ends at the firepit: a recoupling where the girls choose — that’s you, holding the pen. Before that: one more bombshell, and everyone recalculating.

— or, exposed side —

…a recoupling where the girls choose — and you find out what your season is worth.
Week three. It’s quiet. It’s lovely. It ends with a text, a suitcase, and six strangers unpacking aftershave. Nothing survives this week unchanged.
Week four. The fallout settles, new alliances form — and at the end of it, production presses play on everything the cameras saw. Footage outranks feelings.
Week five. One more bombshell, one more recalculation, and a firepit ceremony where the girls hold the pen. The week ends with everyone standing up.

items.context: Final Week opens · the boys’ recoupling, exposed side (trimmed to fit small phones)
items.prompt: “Tonight, there will be a recoupling. The boys will choose. The girl not chosen will be dumped from the Island. #judgementweek” — More girls than places: a maths problem with feelings. No move left tonight. Only what you’ve built — the Connection, or the vote.

items.context: The Final, Win-the-Villa climax (trimmed to fit small phones)
items.prompt: “Islanders. Tonight, the public decide.” — The lawn has a stage now; the stage has fairy lights; the lights, somehow, have a sponsor. Millions of thumbs hover over two names. One is yours. Make the last look count.

items.text: 💘 Coupled up: «Kai» — «vibe». The Connection starts where all Connections start.

💘 The Connection surges. Other couples take it personally.
💘 Real Connection, banked.
💘 The Connection ticks up. Slow is a strategy.
💘 The Connection craters. The duvet has a border now.
💘 A scuff on the Connection. Buffable.

💘 The Connection: enough to hold «Kai»
💘 The Connection: not enough on its own tonight
💘 No Partner, no Connection — the public is the whole plan
💘 Whoever you pick, the Connection starts where all Connections start
Weak Connection, strong fanbase. In ceremony maths, mugs beat hugs. Tonight, anyway.
Recoupling tonight. That Connection of yours is wearing a seatbelt. Should be fine. Should.

items.context: Stirling, first-season tutor at the line-up
items.text: Recoupling rules, quickly: the Connection keeps you, or the nation does. Tonight your Connection’s doing the heavy lifting — as it should.
The Connection blinked. The public didn’t.
💘 Connection is your couple’s strength — The Real Thing runs on it, and it RESETS when you switch partners.

items.context: Daybed Angle blurbs (compounding is per-week now)
items.text:
Comedy Gold: “+8 on challenge and banter choices. Compounds: +1 Charisma every week.”
Everyone’s Type: “+8 on flirt and date choices. Compounds: +1 Charisma every week.”
